concerns.text: The plan defines the background model as 'Peak Regions from *other* cell types' (e.g., testing GM12878 against the union of K562, HepG2, etc.). This creates a circular validation for cell-type specificity. If a motif is enriched in GM12878, it is by definition enriched relative to the 'other' set if that motif is absent in the others. The test becomes a tautology: 'Is this motif more common in set A than in (not A)?' This will mechanically produce high enrichment scores for any motif with non-uniform distribution, failing to distinguish true cell-type-specific regulatory mechanisms from general background noise or ubiquitous motifs. The background must be a matched set of genomic regions (e.g., random genomic windows or ATAC-seq peaks from the *same* cell type matched for GC content and mappability) to test for specific enrichment above the local chromatin accessibility baseline.

The validation metric requires '≥ 60% overlap' between predicted motif peaks and independent ChIP-seq peaks. This threshold is scientifically unsound for TF binding. ChIP-seq peaks represent broad regions (200-1000bp) containing the binding site, while motif scanning identifies short sequence matches (6-20bp). Even for a perfect TF, the motif match will only fall within a fraction of the ChIP-seq peak (often <20-30%). Requiring 60% overlap of the *entire* peak regions (or even the motif matches falling within peaks) is a category error that will almost certainly result in a false negative (null result) for biologically valid interactions, or force the selection of only the most massive, non-specific peaks. The metric should be 'fraction of ChIP-seq peaks containing a motif match' or 'enrichment of motif matches within ChIP-seq peaks vs. background', not a raw overlap percentage of regions.

Using Euclidean distance on -log10(q-values) for hierarchical clustering of cell types is methodologically weak. q-values are bounded [0,1] and transformed, but the magnitude of -log10(q) is heavily influenced by the number of tests and the specific distribution of p-values, not just the biological similarity of the regulatory profiles. Two cell types with similar *patterns* of enrichment but different *strengths* (due to sequencing depth or peak count) will appear distant. A correlation-based distance (e.g., 1 - Pearson/Spearman correlation) on the enrichment scores (log-odds ratios) is the standard in genomics for clustering samples by regulatory profile, as it focuses on the *pattern* of relative enrichment rather than absolute magnitude.
